content.text: is the top-level orchestrator in CrewAI. It binds a list of agents and tasks together, sets the execution strategy, and provides the single entry point —
— that runs everything and returns a
The diagram above shows the three orthogonal concerns a Crew manages: which agents exist, which tasks must run, and how those tasks are scheduled (process).
. Both accept plain lists; order matters for

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

# --- Agents ---
researcher = Agent(
    role="Research Specialist",
    goal="Find accurate, up-to-date information on any topic",
    backstory="A meticulous analyst who values primary sources above all.",
    verbose=True,
)

writer = Agent(
    role="Content Writer",
    goal="Turn raw research into engaging prose",
    backstory="Award-winning tech journalist with a knack for clarity.",
    verbose=True,
)

# --- Tasks ---
research_task = Task(
    description="Research the current state of quantum computing in 2024",
    expected_output="A structured summary with key breakthroughs and players",
    agent=researcher,
)

write_task = Task(
    description="Write a 500-word article based on the research",
    expected_output="A polished article ready for publication",
    agent=writer,
    context=[research_task],  # receives researcher output
)

# --- Crew ---
crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, write_task],
    process=Process.sequential,  # default
    verbose=True,
)

result = crew.kickoff()
print(result.raw)
All agents participating in this crew
Tasks to execute, in order for sequential
LLM for the manager agent (hierarchical only)
Custom manager agent instead of auto-generated
Cache tool results within a run
Called after each agent step with AgentFinish
Called after each task with TaskOutput
Add a planning step before task execution
Tasks run one after another in the order they appear in the
list. Each task can access earlier results through its
list. This is the most predictable mode and suits linear workflows.

crew = Crew(
    agents=[analyst, writer, reviewer],
    tasks=[analysis_task, draft_task, review_task],
    process=Process.sequential,
)
— orchestrates the other agents. It decides which task to assign to which agent, can run tasks in parallel, and synthesises results. Use this when the order of tasks is not fixed in advance.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[gather_task, analyse_task, report_task],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),
    verbose=True,
)

# Provide a custom manager agent instead of auto-generating one
from crewai import Agent
manager = Agent(
    role="Project Manager",
    goal="Deliver a high-quality report",
    backstory="Experienced PM who delegates expertly and never misses a deadline.",
    allow_delegation=True,
)
crew_custom = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[gather_task, analyse_task, report_task],
    process=Process.hierarchical,
    manager_agent=manager,
)
The synchronous entry point. Blocks until all tasks complete and returns a

result = crew.kickoff()

# CrewOutput attributes
print(result.raw)            # last task's final text
print(result.pydantic)       # Pydantic model if last task used output_pydantic
print(result.json_dict)      # dict if last task used output_json
print(result.tasks_output)   # list of TaskOutput, one per task
print(result.token_usage)    # total token counts
Run the same crew multiple times, once per item in a list. Useful for batch processing.

topics = [
    {"topic": "quantum computing"},
    {"topic": "fusion energy"},
    {"topic": "CRISPR therapy"},
]

results = crew.kickoff_for_each(inputs=topics)
for r in results:
    print(r.raw[:200])

import asyncio

async def main():
    result = await crew.kickoff_async(inputs={"topic": "dark matter"})
    print(result.raw)

asyncio.run(main())
to inject runtime values into task descriptions and agent backstories. Wrap placeholders in

researcher = Agent(
    role="{domain} Research Specialist",
    goal="Find data on {topic}",
    backstory="Expert in {domain} with 10 years of experience.",
)

research_task = Task(
    description="Research the latest developments in {topic} for {year}",
    expected_output="A bullet-point summary of key findings",
    agent=researcher,
)

crew = Crew(agents=[researcher], tasks=[research_task], verbose=True)
result = crew.kickoff(inputs={
    "topic": "neuromorphic chips",
    "domain": "hardware engineering",
    "year": "2024",
})
Two callback hooks let you observe execution without subclassing:

from crewai.agents.agent_builder.base_agent_executor_mixin import CrewAgentExecutorMixin

def on_step(step_output):
    # Fires after every agent LLM call
    print(f"[step] {step_output}")

def on_task(task_output):
    # Fires after every task completes
    print(f"[task done] agent={task_output.agent}, chars={len(task_output.raw)}")

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, write_task],
    step_callback=on_step,
    task_callback=on_task,
)
to give the crew a persistent memory store across runs. CrewAI uses embeddings to store and retrieve relevant context automatically. Set
to use a provider other than OpenAI.
, the default) memoises tool results within a single run. The same tool with the same arguments returns the cached result, saving LLM calls for repeated lookups.
to cap the number of LLM requests per minute across the whole crew. This is essential when using API tiers with rate limits.

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[...],
    max_rpm=30,  # caps at 30 requests/min total
)
, CrewAI inserts an extra step before task execution where a planner LLM outlines how to approach the run. This can improve coherence for complex multi-agent workflows.

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, write_task],
    planning=True,
    planning_llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"),  # optional cheaper planner
)
For production use, CrewAI recommends the
decorator pattern. Agents and tasks are declared in YAML; Python wires them together.

# src/config/agents.yaml
researcher:
  role: Research Specialist
  goal: Find accurate information on {topic}
  backstory: Meticulous analyst who values primary sources.

writer:
  role: Content Writer
  goal: Transform research into compelling articles
  backstory: Tech journalist specialising in clarity.

# src/config/tasks.yaml
research_task:
  description: Research {topic} thoroughly
  expected_output: Structured summary with citations
  agent: researcher

write_task:
  description: Write a 600-word article on {topic}
  expected_output: Publication-ready article
  agent: writer
  context:
    - research_task
gives multiple windows into the run:

result = crew.kickoff()

# Text output of the final task
print(result.raw)

# Iterate over all task outputs in execution order
for task_out in result.tasks_output:
    print(f"{task_out.agent}: {task_out.raw[:100]}")

# Token usage across the entire run
usage = result.token_usage
print(f"Total tokens: {usage.total_tokens}")
print(f"  prompt={usage.prompt_tokens}, completion={usage.completion_tokens}")
is the top-level container that binds agents, tasks, and execution strategy.
uses a manager agent to orchestrate and can parallelise work.
injects runtime values into task descriptions and agent prompts.
and async variants enable batch and concurrent execution.
) let you observe every step without subclassing.
) memoises tool calls within a run.
+ YAML is the recommended production pattern for maintainable multi-agent systems.
